title: How cotton linter behaves
subtitle: The fibre characteristics that make cotton-linter paper strong and printable
excerpt: Cotton linter is not the same material as cotton textile fibre, it is shorter, stiffer, and more uniform. These properties produce paper that is unusually strong for its weight, takes ink cleanly without bleed, and ages without the acid decay that shortens the life of wood-pulp papers. Understanding why cotton linter behaves as it does informs every decision in the papermaking process.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923), chapters on the history of rag and cotton paper and their fibre characteristics. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/oldpapermaking00hunt. Cotton-linter chemistry and properties cross-referenced with the Fröbel-era paper craft literature on sheet quality.

content.text: . The strength, colour, surface and shelf life of finished paper all come from the length, purity and chemistry of the fibres used.

sits in a useful middle spot. It is shorter than textile cotton (which makes softer, more see-through sheets). It is longer than wood pulp (which makes shorter-lived paper). And it is naturally acid-free, which makes cotton-linter paper last far longer than wood-pulp paper.

Cotton-linter fibres are 1 to 3 mm long, shorter than the 25 to 30 mm textile cotton fibres that are spun into thread. In a hand-made sheet, fibre length directly influences the number of inter-fibre bonds across the sheet: more bonds per unit area means a stronger sheet for a given weight. Cotton linter, at 1 to 3 mm, forms a dense network of bonds; wood-pulp fibres at 0.5 to 1.5 mm form fewer bonds and weaker sheets. The result is that cotton-linter paper is noticeably stronger than office paper of the same thickness.

Wood pulp contains lignin, a structural polymer that oxidises into acidic compounds as it ages. These acids attack the cellulose, breaking it into shorter and shorter fragments until the paper becomes brittle and brown, a process well advanced in newspapers within twenty years. Cotton linter contains almost no lignin: the fibre is almost pure cellulose with a small amount of wax. Cotton-linter paper stored away from light, moisture, and pollutants can remain stable for centuries. The Bank of England's banknote paper is a cotton-linter sheet.

A freshly formed cotton-linter sheet without sizing is a waterleaf, it absorbs liquid ink immediately and spreads it through the fibre network. For calligraphy and printmaking, the sheet needs sizing to hold ink on the surface. The most traditional sizing method is to dilute gelatin (from sheet gelatin) in warm water and dip the finished dry sheet into the solution, then re-dry under light tension. Alum-rosin sizing is faster but less reversible.

test: dip a fingertip in water and touch the paper surface. On an unsized waterleaf, the droplet absorbs in under two seconds. On a lightly sized calligraphy-grade sheet, the droplet sits on the surface for five to ten seconds before the paper begins to absorb it. A more heavily sized sheet resists absorption for thirty seconds or longer.

A run of twelve A4 cotton-linter sheets pulled at 100 gsm, dried and lightly sized with gelatin, takes iron-gall calligraphy ink with clean sharp edges at a 30° nib angle and a size 3 Mitchell. The result is close to a shop hot-press watercolour paper of the same weight. The same batch left unsized takes Japanese sumi ink well for monotype printing. The ink spreads into the fibres and gives the soft edge that suits that medium.
